content.explanation:
The Django Template Language (DTL) is a lightweight markup language designed for rendering HTML with dynamic data. It provides variables, tags, filters, and comments as its core building blocks. DTL is intentionally limited - it is not a full programming language, which enforces the separation between presentation logic and business logic. Variables are surrounded by double curly braces: {{ variable }}. When the template engine encounters a variable, it evaluates it and replaces it with the result. You can access object attributes and dictionary keys using dot notation: {{ user.username }}, {{ article.title }}, {{ request.GET.page }}. If a variable does not exist, it is silently replaced with an empty string by default. Template tags are surrounded by {% %} and provide logic such as loops, conditionals, and template loading. The most common tags are {% if %}, {% for %}, {% block %}, {% extends %}, {% include %}, and {% url %}. Tags can produce output, control flow, or load external content. Some tags require closing tags ({% endif %}, {% endfor %}), while others are self-contained. Filters modify variables for display and use the pipe character: {{ variable|filter }}. Filters can be chained: {{ text|truncatewords:30|title }}. Common filters include 'default' (fallback value), 'date' (date formatting), 'truncatewords' (word limit), 'title' (title case), 'lower/upper' (case conversion), 'length' (count), 'linebreaks' (newlines to
), 'safe' (mark as safe HTML), and 'pluralize' (add plural suffix). Django auto-escapes all variable output by default to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. This means characters like <, >, &, and quotes are converted to their HTML entity equivalents. If you need to output raw HTML (from a trusted source), use the |safe filter or the {% autoescape off %} tag. Never mark user-provided content as safe. Comments use {# #} for single-line comments and {% comment %} for multi-line comments. Template comments are not included in the rendered output, unlike HTML comments which are visible in the page source. The {% for %} loop provides a special forloop variable with useful attributes: forloop.counter (1-indexed count), forloop.counter0 (0-indexed), forloop.first (boolean), forloop.last (boolean), and forloop.parentloop (for nested loops). The {% empty %} tag inside a for loop handles the case when the list is empty.

codeExamples.title: Variables and Dot Notation
codeExamples.code:
{{ article.title }}

By {{ article.author.username }}

Published: {{ article.published_at }}

Page: {{ request.GET.page }}

{{ article.get_status_display }}

Word count: {{ article.content.split|length }}

{{ user.bio|default:"No bio provided" }}
codeExamples.explanation: Dot notation traverses attributes, dictionary keys, list indexes, and method calls. Methods are called without parentheses and must take no arguments. The |default filter provides a fallback for empty or missing values.

codeExamples.title: Conditional Tags
codeExamples.code:
{% if user.is_authenticated %}

Welcome, {{ user.username }}!

{% elif user_count > 0 %}

Please log in to continue.

{% else %}

No users found.

{% endif %} {% if article.views > 1000 %} Popular {% endif %} {% if user.is_staff and user.is_active %} Admin Panel {% endif %} {% if not article.is_published %} Draft {% endif %} {% if "python" in article.tags.all %} Python Article {% endif %}
codeExamples.explanation: Django templates support if, elif, else with comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), boolean operators (and, or, not), and membership tests (in, not in). Tags must be closed with {% endif %}.

codeExamples.title: For Loops and Empty
codeExamples.code:
{% for article in articles %}
  • {{ forloop.counter }}. {{ article.title }} {% if forloop.first %}(Latest){% endif %}
    • {% empty %}
  • No articles found.
    • {% endfor %}
{% for category in categories %}

{{ category.name }}

    {% for product in category.products.all %}
  • {{ product.name }} - ${{ product.price }}
    • {% endfor %}
{% endfor %}
codeExamples.explanation: {% for %} iterates over sequences. {% empty %} handles empty lists. The forloop variable provides iteration metadata. Nested loops can access the outer loop via forloop.parentloop.

codeExamples.title: Common Filters
codeExamples.code: {{ title|title }} {{ name|lower }} {{ bio|truncatewords:20 }} {{ content|linebreaks }} {{ text|striptags }} {{ slug|slugify }} {{ pub_date|date:"M d, Y" }} {{ pub_date|date:"F j, Y, g:i a" }} {{ pub_date|timesince }} {{ price|floatformat:2 }} {{ count|add:5 }} {{ tags|join:", " }} {{ items|length }} {{ items|first }} {{ items|last }} {{ html_content|safe }} {{ user_input|escape }}
codeExamples.explanation: Filters transform values for display. They are applied with the pipe character and can take arguments after a colon. Filters can be chained: {{ text|truncatewords:20|title }}. The |safe filter should only be used with trusted content.

notes: DTL auto-escapes all variable output by default to prevent XSS attacks.
Use |safe only with trusted content. Never mark user input as safe.
Variables use {{ }}, tags use {% %}, comments use {# #}.
The forloop variable provides counter, first, last, and parentloop attributes.
commonPitfalls: Marking user-provided content as |safe, which opens XSS vulnerabilities.
Trying to call methods with arguments in templates. DTL methods must take no arguments.
Forgetting closing tags ({% endif %}, {% endfor %}). Unclosed tags cause template errors.

exercises.title: Template with Conditionals and Loops
exercises.description: Write a Django template that displays a list of products. For each product, show its name, price formatted to 2 decimal places, and a 'Sale!' badge if the product's on_sale field is True. If the product list is empty, show 'No products available.'
exercises.solution:
{% for product in products %}

{{ product.name }}

${{ product.price|floatformat:2 }}

{% if product.on_sale %} Sale! {% endif %}
{% empty %}

No products available.

{% endfor %}
hints: Use {% for product in products %} to loop
Use {% empty %} inside the for loop for the empty case
Use {{ product.price|floatformat:2 }} for 2 decimal places

exercises.title: Apply Filters
exercises.description:
Write template code that:
1. Displays an article's title in title case
2. Shows the published_at date in 'Month Day, Year' format
3. Truncates the content to 50 words
4. Shows how many comments the article has using the length filter, with the word 'comment' pluralized correctly
exercises.starterCode: <!-- Apply filters to display article data -->
exercises.solution:
{{ article.title|title }}

{{ article.published_at|date:"F j, Y" }}

{{ article.content|truncatewords:50 }}

{{ article.comments.all|length }} comment{{ article.comments.all|length|pluralize }}
hints: Use |title to convert to title case
Date format 'F j, Y' gives 'January 15, 2024'
The |pluralize filter adds 's' when the value is not 1

content.explanation:
Template inheritance is Django's most powerful tool for reducing template duplication. It allows you to build a base 'skeleton' template containing common elements (header, navigation, footer) and define blocks that child templates can override. This follows the DRY principle - common HTML is written once in the base template and inherited by all pages.

The base template uses {% block %} tags to define regions that child templates can fill or override. Each block has a name: {% block content %}{% endblock %}. The base template typically includes the HTML boilerplate, header, navigation, footer, and any shared CSS/JS resources. Blocks can have default content that child templates optionally override.

Child templates use {% extends %} as their very first tag (it must be the first template tag) to specify which template they inherit from. Then they define {% block %} tags to fill in the parent's blocks. Any content outside of block tags in a child template is ignored.

You can access the parent block's content using {{ block.super }}. This is useful when you want to add to the parent's content rather than replace it entirely. For example, a child template might add page-specific CSS to the parent's CSS block using {{ block.super }} followed by additional styles.

Template inheritance can be multi-level. A common pattern is three-level inheritance: base.html (site-wide layout) -> section_base.html (section-specific layout, like blog or shop) -> page.html (individual page content). Each level extends the one above and overrides specific blocks.

The {% include %} tag inserts another template at that point. Unlike {% extends %}, which uses the parent as a skeleton, {% include %} embeds a template fragment. It is ideal for reusable components like navigation bars, card widgets, pagination controls, and form snippets. You can pass context variables to included templates using the 'with' keyword.

Template configuration in settings.py determines where Django looks for templates. DIRS lists directories to search (typically a project-level templates/ directory). APP_DIRS, when True, tells Django to look in each app's templates/ directory. Templates in DIRS take priority over APP_DIRS, allowing project-level templates to override app-level templates.

codeExamples.title: Base Template
codeExamples.code:
{% block extra_css %}{% endblock %}
Home Blog {% if user.is_authenticated %} {{ user.username }} Logout {% else %} Login {% endif %}
{% block content %}{% endblock %}

© 2024 My Site

{% block extra_js %}{% endblock %}
codeExamples.explanation: The base template defines the site-wide HTML structure. {% block %} tags mark regions that child templates can fill. Blocks like extra_css and extra_js allow child templates to inject page-specific resources.

codeExamples.title: Child Template
codeExamples.code:
{% extends 'base.html' %} {% block title %}Blog - My Site{% endblock %} {% block content %}

Blog Posts

{% for article in articles %}

{{ article.title }}

{{ article.content|truncatewords:30 }}

{{ article.published_at|date:"M d, Y" }}
{% empty %}

No articles yet.

{% endfor %} {% if is_paginated %}
{% if page_obj.has_previous %} Previous {% endif %} Page {{ page_obj.number }} of {{ page_obj.paginator.num_pages }} {% if page_obj.has_next %} Next {% endif %}
{% endif %} {% endblock %}
codeExamples.explanation: The child template extends base.html and fills in the title and content blocks. Everything outside block tags is ignored. The parent's header, footer, and boilerplate are inherited automatically.

codeExamples.title: Multi-Level Inheritance with block.super
codeExamples.code:
{% block css %} {% endblock %} {% extends 'base.html' %} {% block css %} {{ block.super }} {% endblock %} {% block content %}
{% block blog_content %}{% endblock %}
{% include 'blog/_sidebar.html' %}
{% endblock %} {% extends 'blog/base_blog.html' %} {% block css %} {{ block.super }} {% endblock %} {% block blog_content %}

{{ article.title }}

{{ article.content|safe }}
{% endblock %}
codeExamples.explanation: Three-level inheritance: base.html -> base_blog.html -> article_detail.html. Each level extends the one above. {{ block.super }} includes the parent's block content, so CSS accumulates: main.css + blog.css + article.css.

codeExamples.title: Include with Context
codeExamples.code:
{{ card_title }}

{{ card_body }}

{% if card_link %} Read more {% endif %}
{% extends 'base.html' %} {% block content %}
{% for article in articles %} {% include 'components/_card.html' with card_title=article.title card_body=article.excerpt card_link=article.get_absolute_url %} {% endfor %} {# Include with 'only' limits context to specified variables #} {% include 'components/_card.html' with card_title="Subscribe" card_body="Get updates" only %}
{% endblock %}
codeExamples.explanation: {% include %} embeds a template fragment. The 'with' keyword passes variables. The 'only' keyword restricts the included template's context to only the specified variables, preventing access to the parent template's context.

notes: {% extends %} must be the first template tag in a child template.
{{ block.super }} includes the parent block's content - useful for additive overriding.
Use {% include %} for reusable components. Use {% extends %} for page-level inheritance.
Template naming convention: prefix partials with underscore (_sidebar.html, _card.html).
interviewTips: Explain the difference between {% extends %} (page-level skeleton) and {% include %} (embedded component).
Describe three-level inheritance: base.html -> section_base.html -> page.html.
Mention {{ block.super }} for additive block overriding.

exercises.title: Create Base and Child Templates
exercises.description: Write a base.html template with blocks for 'title', 'content', and 'extra_js'. Then write a child template 'home.html' that extends base.html, sets the title to 'Home', and displays a welcome message in the content block.
exercises.solution:
{% block content %}{% endblock %}
{% block extra_js %}{% endblock %} {% extends 'base.html' %} {% block title %}Home - My Site{% endblock %} {% block content %}

Welcome to My Site

This is the home page.

{% endblock %}
hints: base.html defines the skeleton with {% block %} tags
The child template starts with {% extends 'base.html' %}
Fill in block tags to override the parent's content

exercises.title: Include a Reusable Component
exercises.description: Write a partial template '_alert.html' that accepts 'alert_type' and 'alert_message' variables and renders a styled div. Then write template code that includes this partial twice: once for a 'success' alert and once for an 'error' alert.
exercises.solution:
{{ alert_message }}

{% include '_alert.html' with alert_type='success' alert_message='Your changes have been saved.' %} {% include '_alert.html' with alert_type='error' alert_message='Something went wrong.' %}
hints: The partial uses variables passed via the 'with' keyword
Use {% include %} to embed the partial
Pass different values for each inclusion

content.explanation: Django provides a rich set of built-in template tags and filters that cover most common presentation needs. Understanding these tools helps you write clean, efficient templates without resorting to complex Python logic in your views. The {% url %} tag generates URLs from named URL patterns, preventing hardcoded URLs. It accepts the URL name and any required parameters: {% url 'blog:post-detail' pk=article.pk %}. For URL namespaces, use the colon-separated format. This tag is essential for maintainable templates - if your URL structure changes, you only update urls.py. The {% static %} tag generates URLs for static files (CSS, JavaScript, images). Before using it, you must load the static template tag library with {% load static %}. Example: . In development, Django serves static files directly. In production, they are typically served by a CDN or web server like Nginx. The {% csrf_token %} tag is mandatory inside all POST forms. It generates a hidden input field with a CSRF token that Django uses to verify the form submission is legitimate and not a cross-site request forgery attack. Forgetting this tag will cause a 403 Forbidden error on form submission. The {% with %} tag assigns a complex expression to a variable for reuse within the block. This is useful when you need to reference the same expensive computation multiple times: {% with total=business.employees.count %}{{ total }} employee{{ total|pluralize }}{% endwith %}. The {% now %} tag displays the current date/time: {% now "Y-m-d" %}. The {% cycle %} tag cycles through values on each iteration, useful for alternating row colors: {% cycle 'odd' 'even' %}. The {% spaceless %} tag removes whitespace between HTML tags. The {% verbatim %} tag prevents template processing, useful when your template contains syntax that conflicts with DTL (like JavaScript template literals). The {% load %} tag loads custom template tag libraries. Django ships with several: {% load static %} for static files, {% load i18n %} for internationalization, {% load humanize %} for human-friendly numbers (intcomma, naturaltime, ordinal). Third-party packages often provide their own template tag libraries. Django's humanize library (django.contrib.humanize) provides especially useful filters: intcomma formats numbers with commas (1000000 -> 1,000,000), naturaltime converts dates to human-readable strings ('3 hours ago'), ordinal adds ordinal suffixes (1 -> '1st', 2 -> '2nd'), and naturalday converts dates to 'today', 'yesterday', or the actual date.

codeExamples.title: URL and Static Tags
codeExamples.code: {% load static %} All Posts {{ article.title }} {{ category.name }} Page 2
codeExamples.explanation: {% url %} generates URLs from named patterns. {% static %} generates URLs for static files. Both prevent hardcoded paths. Remember to {% load static %} at the top of templates using the static tag.

codeExamples.title: CSRF Token and Forms
codeExamples.code:
{% csrf_token %} {{ form.as_p }}
{% csrf_token %}
{{ form.title }} {% if form.title.errors %}
    {% for error in form.title.errors %}
  • {{ error }}
    • {% endfor %}
{% endif %}
{{ form.content }}
{% if form.non_field_errors %}
{% for error in form.non_field_errors %}

{{ error }}

{% endfor %}
{% endif %}
codeExamples.explanation: {% csrf_token %} is mandatory in POST forms to prevent CSRF attacks. Forms can be rendered with form.as_p (automatic), or by accessing individual fields for custom layouts. Always check and display form errors.

codeExamples.title: With, Cycle, and Spaceless Tags
codeExamples.code:
{% with total=products.count %}

{{ total }} product{{ total|pluralize }} found.

{% if total > 100 %}

Showing first 100 results.

{% endif %} {% endwith %} {% for item in items %} {% endfor %}
{{ item.name }}
{% spaceless %}

Home

{% endspaceless %} {% verbatim %}
{{ angularVariable }}
{% raw_template_syntax %} {% endverbatim %}
codeExamples.explanation: {% with %} creates temporary variables to avoid repeating expensive expressions. {% cycle %} alternates values per iteration. {% spaceless %} removes inter-tag whitespace. {% verbatim %} prevents DTL processing.

codeExamples.title: Humanize Filters
codeExamples.code:
{% load humanize %}

{{ product.sales|intcomma }} sales

Posted {{ article.created_at|naturaltime }}

{{ event.date|naturalday }}

{{ user.rank|ordinal }} place

{{ count|apnumber }} items
codeExamples.explanation: Add 'django.contrib.humanize' to INSTALLED_APPS and {% load humanize %} in templates. These filters make numbers and dates more readable for users.

notes: Always use {% url %} for links and {% static %} for static files - never hardcode paths.
{% csrf_token %} is mandatory in POST forms. Omitting it causes a 403 Forbidden error.
{% load %} must be called in each template that uses a tag library - it does not inherit.
Add 'django.contrib.humanize' to INSTALLED_APPS to use humanize filters.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting {% csrf_token %} in POST forms, resulting in 403 errors.
Forgetting {% load static %} before using {% static %} tags.
Assuming {% load %} in a base template applies to child templates. Each template must load its own tag libraries.

exercises.title: Build a Form Template
exercises.description: Write a template that renders a form for creating a blog post. Include: csrf_token, form rendered with individual fields (title and content), error display for each field, and a submit button. The form should POST to the 'blog:post-create' URL.
exercises.solution:
{% csrf_token %}
{{ form.title }} {% for error in form.title.errors %}

{{ error }}

{% endfor %}
{{ form.content }} {% for error in form.content.errors %}

{{ error }}

{% endfor %}
hints: Use {% url 'blog:post-create' %} for the form action
Always include {% csrf_token %} inside the form
Access field errors with form.fieldname.errors

exercises.title: Static Files and Humanize
exercises.description: Write template code that loads the static and humanize libraries, includes a CSS file from 'css/main.css', and displays a product's price with commas, its creation date as natural time, and its sales count as an ordinal.
exercises.starterCode: <!-- Load libraries and display formatted data -->
exercises.solution:
{% load static %} {% load humanize %}

Price: ${{ product.price|intcomma }}

Added {{ product.created_at|naturaltime }}

{{ product.sales_rank|ordinal }} best seller
hints: Use {% load static %} and {% load humanize %} at the top
intcomma adds comma separators to numbers
naturaltime converts dates to '3 hours ago' format

content.explanation:
Django allows you to create custom template tags and filters to extend the template language with your own functionality. This is useful when built-in tags and filters do not cover your specific presentation needs.

Custom tags and filters live in a templatetags/ directory inside your app. This directory must contain an __init__.py file. The Python modules inside templatetags/ become template tag libraries that you load with {% load %}. For example, a file at blog/templatetags/blog_extras.py is loaded with {% load blog_extras %}.

Custom filters are the simplest to create. A filter is a Python function that takes one or two arguments (the value and an optional argument) and returns the transformed value. You register it using the @register.filter decorator. Filters should be pure functions that do not have side effects - they transform a value and return the result.

Simple tags are registered with @register.simple_tag. They can take any number of arguments and return a string. Unlike filters, simple tags can take multiple arguments, access the template context, and perform more complex logic. Use them for generating content that does not fit the filter pattern.

Inclusion tags are the most powerful custom tag type. Registered with @register.inclusion_tag, they render a template with custom context. The function returns a dictionary of context variables, and Django renders the specified template with that context. This is perfect for reusable components like navigation menus, sidebar widgets, or pagination controls that need their own data fetching and rendering logic.

All custom tags and filters must be registered with a template.Library() instance. The convention is to create a 'register' variable at the module level: register = template.Library(). Then use @register.filter, @register.simple_tag, or @register.inclusion_tag as decorators on your functions.

Custom filters can optionally be marked as 'safe' with @register.filter(is_safe=True) if they guarantee their output does not contain unsafe HTML. They can also accept HTML input with @register.filter(needs_autoescape=True), which passes an autoescape parameter that your filter should respect.

codeExamples.title: Custom Filter
codeExamples.explanation: Custom filters are decorated with @register.filter. The first argument is the value being filtered, the optional second argument comes after the colon in the template. The name parameter overrides the function name for use in templates.

codeExamples.title: Simple Tag
codeExamples.explanation: Simple tags can take any number of arguments. takes_context=True gives access to the template context (including request). The 'as' syntax stores the result in a template variable instead of outputting it directly.

codeExamples.title: Inclusion Tag
codeExamples.explanation: Inclusion tags render a separate template with custom context. The function returns a dictionary that becomes the template's context. takes_context=True gives access to the parent template's context. This is ideal for reusable widgets.

codeExamples.title: Directory Structure for Custom Tags
codeExamples.code:
blog/
    __init__.py
    models.py
    views.py
    templatetags/           # Must be a Python package
        __init__.py          # Required (can be empty)
        blog_extras.py       # Your custom tags/filters
        blog_filters.py      # Can have multiple modules
    templates/
        blog/
            _recent_posts.html  # Template for inclusion tag
            _categories.html    # Template for inclusion tag

# In any template:
# {% load blog_extras %}
# {% load blog_filters %}
codeExamples.explanation: The templatetags/ directory must be a Python package (contain __init__.py). Each .py file inside it becomes a loadable template tag library. The app must be in INSTALLED_APPS for Django to discover the templatetags directory.

notes: templatetags/ must be a Python package with __init__.py inside a registered app.
Use @register.filter for value transformations, @register.simple_tag for generating content, @register.inclusion_tag for reusable components.
takes_context=True gives tags access to the template context (request, user, etc.).
The app must be in INSTALLED_APPS for Django to discover its templatetags directory.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to create __init__.py in the templatetags/ directory.
Not registering the app in INSTALLED_APPS. Django will not find templatetags in unregistered apps.
Putting business logic in template tags. Tags should handle presentation, not data manipulation.

exercises.title: Create a Custom Filter
exercises.description: Create a custom filter called 'time_ago' that takes a datetime object and returns a human-readable string like '5 minutes ago', '2 hours ago', or '3 days ago'. Handle minutes, hours, and days.
exercises.starterCode:
from django import template
from django.utils import timezone

register = template.Library()

@register.filter
def time_ago(value):
    # Calculate and return time ago string
    pass
hints: Subtract the value from timezone.now() to get a timedelta
Use total_seconds() to get the difference in seconds
Handle different ranges: <60s, <3600s, <86400s, else

exercises.title: Create an Inclusion Tag
exercises.description: Create an inclusion tag called 'nav_menu' that takes no arguments, queries all Category objects, and renders them using a template called '_nav_menu.html'. Write both the tag function and the template.
exercises.starterCode:
from django import template

register = template.Library()

# Write the inclusion tag
hints: Use @register.inclusion_tag('template_path.html') as the decorator
The function returns a dictionary that becomes the template context
The template renders the data returned by the function
